skill.name: k8s-manifest-generator
skill.description: Generate Kubernetes manifest guidance and templates for Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, Secrets, and storage resources.
skill.summary: Generate production-oriented Kubernetes manifests with security, resource, and validation guidance.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, and secret patterns, but most are documentation examples or Kubernetes YAML templates rather than executable skill code. Confirmed concerns are operational: the skill includes shell hook examples, script ConfigMaps, public LoadBalancer examples, and placeholder Secret material that require human review before use. No evidence found of prompt injection, malicious intent, credential exfiltration, or unauthorized network access by the skill itself.

medium_findings.title: Generated Manifests May Include Shell Commands
medium_findings.description: Several templates and references include shell entry points, lifecycle hooks, command arrays, and script ConfigMaps. These are normal Kubernetes patterns, but copied commands can run inside pods and should be reviewed for privilege, input, and image assumptions before deployment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly present in Kubernetes YAML examples and would execute if copied into manifests. They appear operational rather than malicious because they perform readiness waits, migration commands, health checks, and graceful shutdown behavior.

medium_findings.title: Public Network Exposure Examples Need Review
medium_findings.description: The service templates include internet-facing LoadBalancer examples and a public API pattern with source range 0.0.0.0/0. This is a valid Kubernetes use case, but it can expose workloads publicly if used without policy review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly configure internet-facing or broad public access. The risk is contextual because public APIs can be legitimate, but the pattern requires review before production use.

low_findings.title: Secret Examples Use Placeholder Sensitive Values
low_findings.description: The playbook includes sample Secret values, an API key placeholder, and a private key placeholder. The nearby guidance warns users not to commit plaintext secrets, so this is a documentation risk rather than evidence of embedded real credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sensitive-looking values are visible, but they are placeholders in a documentation block with explicit warnings. No evidence found of real secrets or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto Findings Appear To Be False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner reported weak cryptographic algorithm indicators, but reviewed examples at the cited areas are Kubernetes descriptions, annotations, labels, rollout fields, or documentation headings. No evidence found of code using weak cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed cited locations do not contain cryptographic operations. The alerts appear to match substrings in words such as description or Kubernetes metadata examples.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs And IP Addresses Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Hardcoded hosts, URLs, and IP ranges appear in service discovery examples, documentation links, placeholder backend names, and reserved documentation networks. They are not used for outbound calls by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network indicators are in YAML or markdown examples, not executable downloader or exfiltration logic. Some examples still deserve user review because generated manifests can configure real network exposure.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Commands In Kubernetes Hooks And Scripts
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill may generate manifests containing shell commands such as init scripts, readiness loops, lifecycle hooks, and kubectl validation commands. This pattern is acceptable for Kubernetes guidance but should not be treated as automatically safe.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command patterns are directly present and semantically relevant to the skill output. They are not hidden or obfuscated, and they are used as examples.

dangerous_patterns.title: Public LoadBalancer Configuration Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes public Service examples, including an internet-facing load balancer and unrestricted source ranges. Users should restrict access according to cluster and cloud policy.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The public exposure pattern is explicit in the documentation. It is a legitimate configuration option but can create real exposure if copied without review.

content.value_statement: Teams need repeatable Kubernetes YAML that includes security settings, probes, resources, and networking choices. This skill provides structured guidance and templates for production-oriented manifests.
actual_capabilities: Creates Deployment guidance with replicas, probes, resource limits, labels, and security contexts.
Explains Service patterns for ClusterIP, LoadBalancer, NodePort, Headless, and ExternalName resources.
Provides ConfigMap examples for key-value settings, configuration files, scripts, and Prometheus configuration.
Documents Secret usage patterns and recommends external secret management for sensitive values.
Includes validation steps using kubectl dry runs and common Kubernetes linting tools.
Covers multi-environment organization with single files, separated manifests, and Kustomize layouts.
limitations: It does not connect to a Kubernetes cluster or apply manifests for the user.
It cannot verify cloud-provider annotations against a live account or cluster policy.
Generated examples require review before production use, especially networking and Secret resources.
It focuses on core Kubernetes manifests, not full Helm chart generation.

use_cases.title: Create a secure web service deployment
use_cases.description: Build Deployment and Service manifests with probes, resource limits, non-root execution, and controlled rollout settings.

use_cases.title: Standardize application configuration
use_cases.description: Generate ConfigMap and Secret patterns that separate non-sensitive settings from credentials and certificates.

use_cases.title: Prepare Kubernetes onboarding examples
use_cases.description: Create reference manifests and validation steps for teams learning consistent Kubernetes deployment practices.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Deployment
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a simple workload manifest
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Kubernetes Deployment for my web app. Use image example.com/myapp:1.2.3, port 8080, three replicas, and basic health checks.

prompt_templates.title: Deployment and Service Pair
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a Deployment and ClusterIP Service for an internal API. Include labels, resource requests, resource limits, readiness checks, and liveness checks.

prompt_templates.title: Config and Secret Layout
prompt_templates.prompt: Design ConfigMap and Secret manifests for a production service. Use ConfigMap for non-sensitive settings and Secret references for credentials.

prompt_templates.title: Production Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Kubernetes manifest plan for production readiness. Check security contexts, public exposure, resource limits, probes, rollout strategy, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: A team needs a production web API manifest with port 8080 and three replicas.
output_examples.output: The skill produces a Deployment plan with labels, non-root security context, resource requests, resource limits, readiness checks, liveness checks, and a matching Service recommendation.

output_examples.input: A developer needs to manage database host settings and API credentials.
output_examples.output: The skill separates non-sensitive configuration into a ConfigMap and places credential references in a Secret pattern with guidance to use an external secret manager.

output_examples.input: A platform team wants to expose a public HTTPS API.
output_examples.output: The skill outlines a LoadBalancer Service pattern, highlights source range restrictions, and calls for cloud-specific annotation review before deployment.

best_practices: Review generated public networking settings before applying any Service manifest.
Replace placeholder values and manage real secrets with sealed or external secret tooling.
Validate manifests with dry runs and policy checks before committing or deploying them.
anti_patterns: Do not publish generated Secret values or private keys in source control.
Do not expose LoadBalancer services to all source ranges without an explicit approval.
Do not copy shell hooks into production manifests without reviewing command behavior and permissions.

faq.question: Does this skill apply manifests to a cluster?
faq.answer: No. It provides manifest guidance and examples. Users must apply and validate manifests with their own Kubernetes tools.

faq.question: Can it generate Deployments and Services together?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for matching labels, ports, probes, and service types for common application patterns.

faq.question: Does it handle Secrets safely?
faq.answer: It documents Secret patterns and warns against committing plaintext secrets. Real credentials should use sealed or external secret systems.

faq.question: Can it support public LoadBalancer services?
faq.answer: Yes, but public exposure must be reviewed. Source ranges, TLS, and cloud annotations should match the organization policy.

faq.question: Is Helm chart generation included?
faq.answer: No. The skill focuses on raw Kubernetes manifests and references Helm or Kustomize as next steps.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
